NTPC.q: Which city is known as the Silicon Valley of India?
NTPC.hint: It is the capital of Karnataka.
NTPC.solution: Bengaluru is known as the Silicon Valley of India.

NTPC.q: Who was the first woman PM of India?

NTPC.q: Which city is known as the 'Heritage City' of Karnataka?
NTPC.solution: Mysore is known as the Heritage City of Karnataka.

NTPC.solution: The first session was held at Gokuldas Tejpal Sanskrit College, Bombay.

NTPC.hint: A prominent freedom fighter and poet.

NTPC.q: Which article of the Indian Constitution deals with the 'Right to Education'?

NTPC.hint: Similar size and density to Earth.

NTPC.solution: ISRO headquarters is in Bengaluru (Antariksh Bhavan).

NTPC.solution: Lord Mountbatten was the first; C. Rajagopalachari was the first Indian G-G.

NTPC.q: The 'Dual Government' system in Bengal was abolished by:
NTPC.hint: He was the first Governor-General of Bengal.

NTPC.q: Which Indian state has the longest coastline?
NTPC.solution: Gujarat has the longest coastline in India.

NTPC.q: Who wrote the Indian National Anthem 'Jana Gana Mana'?
NTPC.solution: Rabindranath Tagore composed Jana Gana Mana, adopted as national anthem in 1950.

NTPC.q: Which is the largest state in India by area?

NTPC.solution: The Taj Mahal is located in Agra, Uttar Pradesh, built by Shah Jahan.

NTPC.q: Which is the longest river in India?
NTPC.solution: The Ganga is the longest river in India at about 2,525 km.

NTPC.q: Who is known as the 'Father of the Nation' in India?
NTPC.solution: Mahatma Gandhi is called the Father of the Nation for leading independence movement.

NTPC.q: In which year did India gain Independence?
NTPC.solution: India gained independence on August 15, 1947 from British rule.

NTPC.q: Which is the national animal of India?
NTPC.hint: Project was launched for its conservation.

NTPC.q: Which is the smallest state in India by area?
NTPC.solution: Goa is the smallest state in India by area.

NTPC.q: The Indian Constitution came into effect on:
NTPC.solution: The Constitution was enforced on January 26, 1950, celebrated as Republic Day.

NTPC.q: Which planet is called the Red Planet?
NTPC.hint: Has iron oxide on its surface.
NTPC.solution: Mars appears red due to iron oxide (rust) on its surface.

NTPC.q: Who was the first President of India?
NTPC.solution: Dr. Rajendra Prasad served as the first President of India (1950–1962).

NTPC.q: The 'Dandi March' of 1930 was associated with:
NTPC.solution: Gandhi led the Dandi March to protest the British Salt Tax.

NTPC.q: How many languages are recognized in the 8th Schedule of the Indian Constitution?
NTPC.solution: The 8th Schedule currently recognizes 22 official languages.

NTPC.q: Which article of the Indian Constitution abolished untouchability?
NTPC.solution: Article 17 abolishes untouchability and its practice is an offense.

NTPC.q: The headquarters of the United Nations is in:
NTPC.solution: The UN Headquarters is located in New York City, USA.

NTPC.q: Which mountain range separates India from China?
NTPC.solution: The Himalayas form the northern boundary separating India from China and Tibet.

NTPC.solution: Rakesh Sharma flew aboard Soyuz T-11 in 1984, the first Indian in space.

NTPC.q: The 'Green Revolution' in India is associated with which crop?
NTPC.solution: The Green Revolution primarily boosted wheat production in India in the 1960s–70s.

NTPC.q: Which ocean is the smallest in the world?
NTPC.solution: The Arctic Ocean is the smallest and shallowest of the world's oceans.

NTPC.q: Who founded the city of Agra?
NTPC.solution: Agra was founded by Sikandar Lodi in 1504 as his capital.

NTPC.q: The Durand Line is the boundary between:

NTPC.q: Project Tiger was launched in India in:
NTPC.solution: Project Tiger was launched in 1973 to protect Bengal tigers.

NTPC.q: Which is the highest civilian award of India?
NTPC.solution: The Bharat Ratna is India's highest civilian honour, instituted in 1954.

NTPC.q: The Jallianwala Bagh massacre took place in:
NTPC.solution: The massacre occurred on April 13, 1919 when British troops fired on civilians.

NTPC.q: Which river flows through the Jim Corbett National Park?
NTPC.solution: The Ramganga river flows through Jim Corbett National Park.

NTPC.q: Which Mughal emperor built the Red Fort in Delhi?
NTPC.solution: Shah Jahan built the Red Fort between 1638 and 1648.

NTPC.q: The 'Poona Pact' of 1932 was signed between Gandhi and:
NTPC.solution: Gandhi and Ambedkar signed Poona Pact reserving seats for Depressed Classes.

NTPC.q: Which constitutional amendment reduced the voting age from 21 to 18?
NTPC.solution: The 61st Amendment Act (1988) reduced voting age to 18 years.

NTPC.q: Who gave the slogan 'Jai Jawan Jai Kisan'?
NTPC.solution: Lal Bahadur Shastri gave this slogan during the 1965 Indo-Pakistan war.

NTPC.hint: Also first GG of free India.
NTPC.solution: Lord Mountbatten was Viceroy and became first Governor-General of independent India.

NTPC.q: The 'Directive Principles of State Policy' in India are borrowed from:
NTPC.solution: DPSPs are borrowed from the Irish Constitution of 1937.

NTPC.q: Which river forms the boundary between India and Pakistan in Punjab?
NTPC.solution: The Ravi river forms part of the India-Pakistan boundary in Punjab.

NTPC.q: In which state is the Kaziranga National Park located?
NTPC.solution: Kaziranga National Park is in Assam, a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

NTPC.q: The first Five Year Plan in India covered the period:
NTPC.solution: The First Five Year Plan ran from 1951 to 1956, focusing on agriculture.

NTPC.q: Which of these is not a Fundamental Right in India?
NTPC.solution: Right to Property was removed from Fundamental Rights by 44th Amendment, 1978.

NTPC.solution: Lord Wellesley introduced Subsidiary Alliance in 1798 to extend British control.

NTPC.q: Which commission was set up to review centre-state relations in India?
NTPC.solution: Sarkaria Commission (1983) reviewed centre-state relations and submitted report in 1988.

NTPC.q: Which is the capital city of Australia?
NTPC.solution: Canberra is the capital of Australia, chosen as a compromise between Sydney and Melbourne.

NTPC.q: How many bones are there in an adult human body?
NTPC.solution: An adult human body has 206 bones.

NTPC.q: Which is the largest ocean in the world?
NTPC.solution: The Pacific Ocean is the largest, covering about 165 million square kilometres.

NTPC.q: The term 'zero hour' in Parliament refers to:
NTPC.solution: Zero Hour is the time (12 noon) when MPs raise urgent public matters without prior notice.

NTPC.q: Which gas is responsible for the greenhouse effect primarily?
NTPC.solution: CO2 is the primary greenhouse gas responsible for global warming.

NTPC.q: Operation Flood was related to:
NTPC.solution: Operation Flood (1970) was the world's largest dairy development programme.

NTPC.q: The Tropic of Cancer passes through how many Indian states?
NTPC.solution: Tropic of Cancer passes through 8 Indian states including Gujarat, MP, and WB.

NTPC.q: Which Indian state has the largest number of districts?
NTPC.solution: Uttar Pradesh has the largest number of districts (75) in India.

NTPC.solution: Lord Dalhousie introduced Doctrine of Lapse to annex states without natural heirs.

NTPC.q: Which Schedule of the Indian Constitution contains the Anti-Defection Law?
NTPC.solution: The 10th Schedule (added 1985) deals with disqualification on grounds of defection.

NTPC.q: The Balwant Rai Mehta Committee (1957) was related to:
NTPC.solution: Balwant Rai Mehta Committee recommended establishment of Panchayati Raj institutions.

NTPC.q: Which writ is issued to produce a person before a court?
NTPC.solution: Habeas Corpus is issued to bring a detained person before the court.

NTPC.q: Which sport is Sachin Tendulkar associated with?
NTPC.solution: Sachin Tendulkar is a legendary Indian cricketer with 100 international centuries.

NTPC.q: The currency of Japan is:
NTPC.solution: The Yen (¥) is the official currency of Japan.

NTPC.q: India's first satellite was named:
NTPC.solution: Aryabhata was India's first satellite, launched in 1975 by the USSR.

NTPC.q: The 'Geneva Convention' deals with:
NTPC.solution: Geneva Conventions establish standards for humane treatment of POWs and civilians.

NTPC.q: Which country is known as the 'Land of Rising Sun'?
NTPC.solution: Japan is called Land of the Rising Sun because it lies to the east where the sun rises first.

NTPC.q: The 'Chipko Movement' was related to:
NTPC.solution: Chipko Movement (1973) in Uttarakhand protested deforestation by hugging trees.

NTPC.q: Which bank was established first in India?
NTPC.solution: Bank of Hindustan (1770) was the first bank in India, though it closed in 1832.

NTPC.solution: Judicial Review concept is borrowed from the USA constitution.

NTPC.q: What is the capital of France?
NTPC.solution: Paris is the capital and largest city of France.

NTPC.q: What is the full form of UNO?
NTPC.solution: UNO stands for United Nations Organization, established after WWII.

NTPC.q: NABARD is related to which sector?
NTPC.solution: NABARD (National Bank for Agriculture and Rural Development) was set up in 1982.

NTPC.q: Which dam is the highest in India?
NTPC.solution: Tehri Dam is the highest dam in India at 260.5 metres.

NTPC.solution: The Regulating Act was the first step by British Parliament to control East India Company.

NTPC.q: Who established the Asiatic Society of Bengal?
NTPC.solution: Sir William Jones founded the Asiatic Society of Bengal in 1784 in Calcutta.

NTPC.q: The Indian National Army (INA) was organized by:
NTPC.solution: Netaji Subhas Chandra Bose organized the INA (Azad Hind Fauj) during WWII.

NTPC.q: Which constitutional body conducts elections in India?
NTPC.solution: The Election Commission of India, under Article 324, supervises all elections.

NTPC.q: Which is the national flower of India?
NTPC.solution: Lotus (Nelumbo nucifera) is the national flower of India.

NTPC.q: What is the speed of light (approximately)?
NTPC.solution: Speed of light is approximately 3×10⁸ metres per second in vacuum.

NTPC.q: Which country gifted the Statue of Liberty to the USA?
NTPC.solution: France gifted the Statue of Liberty to the United States in 1886.

NTPC.q: Who was the last Viceroy of India?
NTPC.hint: Also the first GG of free India.
NTPC.solution: Lord Mountbatten was the last Viceroy of British India (1947).

NTPC.q: The word 'Satyameva Jayate' is taken from:
NTPC.solution: 'Satyameva Jayate' (Truth alone triumphs) is from the Mundaka Upanishad.

NTPC.solution: Lord Cornwallis introduced the Permanent Settlement with zamindars of Bengal in 1793.

NTPC.q: What is the full form of NASA?
NTPC.solution: NASA stands for National Aeronautics and Space Administration.

NTPC.q: Which is the tallest mountain in the world?

NTPC.q: India shares its longest border with which country?
NTPC.solution: India shares its longest border (~4,156 km) with Bangladesh.

NTPC.solution: Narmada is one of the few rivers flowing west and drains into the Arabian Sea.

NTPC.hint: Year before Constitution came into force.

NTPC.solution: Sarojini Naidu was the first woman Governor, serving as Governor of UP (1947–49).

NTPC.solution: Vande Bharat Express (Train 18) was introduced in February 2019.

NTPC.solution: Vitamin D is called the Sunshine Vitamin as skin produces it when exposed to sunlight.

NTPC.solution: The Western Ghats (Sahyadri) runs parallel to the western coast of India.

NTPC.q: World Environment Day is celebrated on:

NTPC.q: Article 360 of the Indian Constitution deals with:

NTPC.q: The Indus Valley Civilization flourished around:

NTPC.q: Who invented the telephone?

NTPC.q: What does SEBI stand for?
options: Securities and Exchange Board of India

NTPC.q: Which is the capital of India?
NTPC.solution: New Delhi is the capital of India and seat of all three branches of government.

NTPC.solution: A cricket team consists of 11 players.

NTPC.q: Which is the national bird of India?
NTPC.solution: The Indian Peacock (Pavo cristatus) is the national bird of India.

NTPC.solution: A leap year has 366 days, with February having 29 days.

NTPC.q: What is the chemical symbol of gold?

NTPC.solution: The Battle of Plassey was fought in 1757, establishing British supremacy in Bengal.

NTPC.solution: India is described as a Sovereign, Socialist, Secular, Democratic Republic in the Preamble.

NTPC.solution: Simon Commission arrived in India in 1928 and was boycotted with 'Simon Go Back' protests.

NTPC.q: The headquarters of the World Health Organization (WHO) is in:
NTPC.solution: WHO is headquartered in Geneva, Switzerland.

NTPC.solution: Kerala is called the Spice Garden of India due to its rich spice production.

NTPC.solution: India conducted its first nuclear test 'Smiling Buddha' at Pokhran, Rajasthan in 1974.

NTPC.solution: Lokpal is equivalent to the Ombudsman — an independent body to investigate complaints against public officials.

NTPC.q: The headquarters of ISRO is located in:
NTPC.solution: ISRO (Indian Space Research Organisation) headquarters is in Bengaluru, Karnataka.

NTPC.q: Which is the largest lake in India?

NTPC.q: The 'Quit India Movement' was launched in:
NTPC.solution: The Quit India Movement was launched on August 8, 1942 by Mahatma Gandhi.

NTPC.q: Who is known as the 'Iron Man of India'?
NTPC.solution: Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel is called Iron Man of India for integrating 562 princely states.

NTPC.q: The 'Sundarbans' delta is shared between India and:
NTPC.solution: The Sundarbans mangrove delta is shared between India (West Bengal) and Bangladesh.

NTPC.q: How many judges are there in the Supreme Court of India (including CJI)?
NTPC.solution: The Supreme Court of India has 34 judges including the Chief Justice of India.

NTPC.solution: Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala (1973) established the Basic Structure doctrine of the Constitution.

NTPC.q: The Tashkent Agreement (1966) was signed after the:
NTPC.solution: Tashkent Agreement was signed after the 1965 Indo-Pakistan war; PM Shastri died the next day.

NTPC.q: Article 32 of the Constitution is related to:
NTPC.hint: Ambedkar called it the 'heart and soul'.

NTPC.q: The 'Nalanda University' was established by which Gupta ruler?
NTPC.solution: Kumaragupta I founded Nalanda University in the 5th century CE, Bihar.

NTPC.q: Who gave the concept of 'Panchsheel' (Five Principles of Peaceful Coexistence)?
NTPC.solution: Panchsheel principles were jointly enunciated by Nehru and Chinese PM Zhou Enlai in 1954.

NTPC.q: The Swadeshi Movement was a part of which agitation?
NTPC.solution: Swadeshi Movement arose as a protest against the Partition of Bengal in 1905.

NTPC.q: Which is the highest gallantry award in India during peacetime?
NTPC.hint: Equivalent of Param Vir Chakra in peacetime.
NTPC.solution: Ashoka Chakra is India's highest peacetime gallantry award, equivalent to Param Vir Chakra.

NTPC.q: Which planet has rings around it?
NTPC.hint: Most prominent rings in the solar system.

NTPC.q: The study of maps is called:
NTPC.solution: Cartography is the study and practice of making and reading maps.

NTPC.q: The 'Indira Gandhi Canal' (Rajasthan Canal) draws water from:
NTPC.solution: The Indira Gandhi Canal draws water from the Sutlej and Beas rivers at the Harike Barrage.

NTPC.q: Which mineral is found in abundance in the Jharkhand state?

NTPC.q: The 'Gandhara School of Art' flourished under which rulers?
NTPC.solution: Gandhara art flourished under the Kushana rulers, blending Greek and Buddhist art.

NTPC.q: The Governor of a state is appointed by the:
NTPC.solution: The Governor of a state is appointed by the President of India under Article 155.

NTPC.q: Which is the smallest ocean in the world?
NTPC.solution: The Arctic Ocean is the smallest and shallowest of Earth's five oceans.

NTPC.q: The 'Jhum cultivation' is a form of:
NTPC.solution: Jhum is a form of shifting (slash-and-burn) cultivation practiced in Northeast India.

NTPC.solution: Kautilya (Chanakya) wrote the Arthashastra, an ancient Indian treatise on statecraft and economics.

NTPC.q: India's Planning Commission was replaced by which body in 2015?
NTPC.solution: NITI Aayog (National Institution for Transforming India) replaced the Planning Commission in 2015.

NTPC.q: The 'Montagu-Chelmsford Reforms' of 1919 introduced:
options: Bicameral legislature at centre first time
NTPC.solution: Montagu-Chelmsford Reforms (GOI Act 1919) introduced diarchy in provinces — dual governance.

NTPC.q: Which committee recommended the three-tier Panchayati Raj system?

NTPC.q: The first session of the Indian National Congress was held in:

NTPC.q: The 'Young Bengal Movement' was associated with:
NTPC.solution: Henry Louis Vivian Derozio inspired the Young Bengal Movement promoting free thinking in the 1820s.

NTPC.q: The concept of 'Welfare State' is enshrined in which part of the Indian Constitution?

NTPC.q: What is the SI unit of pressure?
NTPC.solution: Pascal (Pa) is the SI unit of pressure. 1 Pa = 1 N/m².

NTPC.q: The Tropic of Cancer passes through which city in India?

NTPC.q: Which article of the Indian Constitution deals with impeachment of the President?

NTPC.solution: National Waterway 1 runs along the Ganga from Allahabad (Prayagraj) to Haldia — 1,620 km.

NTPC.q: Which dynasty built the Ajanta Caves?

NTPC.q: The 'Rajya Sabha' is a permanent body because:
options: Its members are elected for 6 years
It cannot be dissolved and 1/3 members retire every 2 years
NTPC.solution: Rajya Sabha cannot be dissolved; 1/3 of its members retire every two years.

NTPC.q: The 'Widow Remarriage Act' of 1856 was passed due to efforts of:
NTPC.solution: Ishwar Chandra Vidyasagar's relentless efforts led to the Hindu Widows' Remarriage Act of 1856.

NTPC.q: The 'Subsidiary Alliance' signed with which state first?

NTPC.solution: Earth is an oblate spheroid — slightly flattened at the poles and bulging at the equator.

NTPC.q: The 'International Court of Justice' is located in:

NTPC.q: The 'Champaran Satyagraha' (1917) was related to:
NTPC.solution: Champaran Satyagraha (1917) was Gandhi's first in India, against forced indigo cultivation in Bihar.

NTPC.q: Which schedule of the Indian Constitution deals with the allocation of seats in the Rajya Sabha?
NTPC.solution: The Fourth Schedule of the Indian Constitution allocates seats in the Rajya Sabha to states and UTs.

NTPC.q: Who introduced the 'Vernacular Press Act' of 1878?
NTPC.solution: Lord Lytton introduced the Vernacular Press Act in 1878 to muzzle the Indian language press.

NTPC.q: The 'Comptroller and Auditor General of India' is appointed by:
NTPC.solution: The CAG is appointed by the President of India under Article 148 of the Constitution.

NTPC.q: Which ocean is to the east of India?
NTPC.solution: The Bay of Bengal lies to the east of India, bordered by the eastern coastline.

NTPC.q: What does NASA's ISRO equivalent stand for?
NTPC.solution: ISRO stands for Indian Space Research Organisation, established in 1969.

NTPC.q: The 'Ghadar Party' was founded in:
NTPC.solution: The Ghadar Party was founded in San Francisco in 1913 by Lala Har Dayal.

NTPC.q: The 'Berubari Union case' is related to:
NTPC.solution: Berubari Union case dealt with cession of Indian territory (West Bengal) to Pakistan.

NTPC.q: Who established the 'Ramakrishna Mission'?
NTPC.solution: Swami Vivekananda established the Ramakrishna Mission in 1897 in memory of his guru.

NTPC.q: India's largest port by traffic volume is:
NTPC.solution: Jawaharlal Nehru Port Trust (JNPT) near Mumbai handles the largest container traffic in India.

NTPC.q: The 'Pitt's India Act' of 1784 established:
NTPC.solution: Pitt's India Act 1784 established a Board of Control giving the British Crown supervision over the East India Company.

NTPC.q: The 'Constituent Assembly' of India was formed in:
NTPC.solution: The Constituent Assembly of India was formed in December 1946 under the Cabinet Mission Plan.

NTPC.q: Which is the oldest mountain range in India?
NTPC.hint: One of the oldest in the world.
NTPC.solution: The Aravalli Range is one of the world's oldest mountain ranges, about 350 million years old.

NTPC.q: The 'National Emergency' in India has been proclaimed how many times?
NTPC.solution: National Emergency has been proclaimed three times — 1962 (China war), 1971 (Pakistan war), 1975 (internal disturbance).

NTPC.q: India is divided into how many time zones?
NTPC.hint: IST is the only time zone.
NTPC.solution: India uses a single time zone — Indian Standard Time (IST), UTC+5:30.

NTPC.q: The 'Padma Awards' are announced on:
NTPC.solution: Padma Awards are announced on the eve of Republic Day (January 26) every year.

NTPC.q: The term 'Secular' in the Indian Constitution means:
NTPC.solution: Secular means India has no state religion; all religions are treated equally by the state.

NTPC.q: The 'Deccan Plateau' is bound on the east by:
NTPC.solution: The Eastern Ghats form the eastern boundary of the Deccan Plateau.

NTPC.q: The 'Morley-Minto Reforms' of 1909 introduced:
NTPC.hint: Lord Minto accepted Aga Khan's demand.
NTPC.solution: Morley-Minto Reforms (1909) introduced separate electorates for Muslims — a divisive step.

NTPC.q: The 'Sixth Schedule' of the Indian Constitution deals with:
NTPC.solution: Sixth Schedule provides for autonomous district councils for tribal administration in Assam, Meghalaya, Tripura, and Mizoram.

NTPC.q: The Sariska National Park is located in:
NTPC.solution: Sariska National Park is located in Alwar district, Rajasthan.

NTPC.q: Which gas do plants absorb during photosynthesis?
NTPC.hint: Used with sunlight to make food.
NTPC.solution: Plants absorb carbon dioxide (CO₂) during photosynthesis to produce glucose.

NTPC.q: The 'Swaraj Party' was founded in 1923 by:
NTPC.solution: Swaraj Party was founded by C.R. Das and Motilal Nehru in 1923 to contest council elections.

NTPC.q: The highest point of the Eastern Ghats is:
NTPC.solution: Arma Konda (1,680 m) in Andhra Pradesh is the highest point of the Eastern Ghats.

NTPC.q: Who was the Chairman of the Drafting Committee of the Indian Constitution?
NTPC.solution: Dr. B.R. Ambedkar chaired the Drafting Committee of the Indian Constitution.

NTPC.q: The 'Indian Independence Act' was passed by the British Parliament in:
NTPC.solution: The Indian Independence Act was passed in July 1947, coming into effect on August 15, 1947.

NTPC.q: The 'Lahore Resolution' of 1940 demanded:
NTPC.solution: The Lahore Resolution (March 1940) by the Muslim League demanded separate nation(s) for Muslims.

NTPC.q: Which city is known as the 'Pink City' of India?
NTPC.solution: Jaipur is called the Pink City because many buildings were painted pink to welcome Prince Albert in 1876.

NTPC.q: The 'National Human Rights Commission' of India was established in:
NTPC.solution: NHRC was established in October 1993 under the Protection of Human Rights Act, 1993.

NTPC.q: Which river is known as the 'Dakshin Ganga'?
NTPC.solution: Godavari is called Dakshin Ganga (Ganga of the South) — longest peninsular river at 1,465 km.

NTPC.q: The 'Chipko Movement' was started in which district of Uttarakhand?
NTPC.solution: The Chipko Movement started in 1973 in Chamoli district, Uttarakhand.

NTPC.q: The 'National Parks' in India were established under:
NTPC.solution: National Parks in India are established and governed under the Wildlife Protection Act, 1972.

NTPC.q: The concept of 'Zero-Based Budgeting' was introduced in India in:
NTPC.solution: Zero-Based Budgeting was introduced in India in 1986 during Rajiv Gandhi's government.

NTPC.q: The 'Lakshadweep' islands are located in which ocean?
NTPC.solution: Lakshadweep is a group of coral islands located in the Arabian Sea.

NTPC.q: Which is the national flower of India?
NTPC.solution: Lotus (Nelumbo nucifera) is the national flower of India, symbolising purity and enlightenment.

NTPC.q: How many states are there in India (as of 2024)?
NTPC.solution: India has 28 states and 8 Union Territories after J&K was bifurcated into two UTs in 2019.

NTPC.q: Which is the smallest state of India by area?
NTPC.solution: Goa is the smallest state of India by area, covering about 3,702 sq km.

NTPC.q: The national anthem of India was composed by:
NTPC.solution: Jana Gana Mana, the national anthem of India, was composed by Rabindranath Tagore.

NTPC.q: Which planet is nearest to the Sun?
NTPC.solution: Mercury is the nearest planet to the Sun, orbiting at an average distance of 57.9 million km.

NTPC.q: The Constitution of India came into effect on:
NTPC.solution: The Constitution of India came into effect on 26 January 1950, celebrated as Republic Day.

NTPC.q: Which is the largest desert in the world?
NTPC.solution: The Sahara Desert in Africa is the world's largest hot desert, covering about 9.2 million sq km.

NTPC.q: Who was the first woman Prime Minister of India?
NTPC.solution: Indira Gandhi was India's first (and only) female Prime Minister, serving from 1966 to 1977 and 1980 to 1984.

NTPC.q: The River Ganga originates from:
NTPC.solution: The Ganga originates from the Gangotri Glacier in Uttarakhand at a place called Gomukh.

NTPC.q: Which country gifted the Statue of Liberty to the USA?
NTPC.solution: France gifted the Statue of Liberty to the USA in 1886 as a symbol of friendship.

NTPC.q: Which is the currency of Japan?
NTPC.solution: The Yen (¥) is the official currency of Japan.

NTPC.q: The Rajya Sabha is also known as:
NTPC.solution: Rajya Sabha is the Council of States — the upper house of India's Parliament.

NTPC.q: Which gas is known as 'laughing gas'?
NTPC.solution: Nitrous oxide (N₂O) is called laughing gas because it causes euphoria when inhaled.

NTPC.q: The headquarters of the United Nations is located in:
NTPC.solution: The UN headquarters is located in New York City, USA, on the East Side of Manhattan.

NTPC.q: Which instrument is used to measure blood pressure?
NTPC.solution: A sphygmomanometer is used to measure blood pressure, typically expressed as systolic/diastolic (e.g., 120/80 mmHg).

NTPC.q: The 'Rowlatt Act' of 1919 was protested because it:
NTPC.solution: The Rowlatt Act allowed the British to imprison Indians without trial, sparking nationwide protests led by Gandhi.

NTPC.q: Who was the first President of India?
NTPC.solution: Dr. Rajendra Prasad was the first President of India (1950–1962).

NTPC.q: The 'Dandi March' covered a distance of approximately:
NTPC.solution: The Dandi March (Salt March) of 1930 covered 241 miles (388 km) from Sabarmati Ashram to Dandi.

NTPC.q: Which article of the Constitution abolishes untouchability?
NTPC.solution: Article 17 of the Indian Constitution abolishes 'untouchability' in any form.

NTPC.q: The 'Green Revolution' in India is associated with:
NTPC.solution: Norman Borlaug and M.S. Swaminathan are credited with the Green Revolution in India in the 1960s.

NTPC.q: Which Fundamental Right is available only to Indian citizens (not foreigners)?
NTPC.solution: Article 19 rights (freedom of speech, assembly, movement etc.) are available only to Indian citizens.

NTPC.q: The 'Regulating Act' of 1773 was significant because it:
options: Established the first written constitution for British India
Established the Supreme Court at Calcutta
NTPC.solution: The Regulating Act 1773 established the Supreme Court at Calcutta and created the post of Governor-General.

NTPC.q: India's first satellite, Aryabhata, was launched in:
NTPC.solution: Aryabhata, India's first satellite, was launched on 19 April 1975 by the Soviet Union.

NTPC.q: The 'Durand Line' is the international boundary between:
NTPC.solution: The Durand Line is the 2,670 km border between Pakistan and Afghanistan, drawn by Sir Henry Durand in 1893.

NTPC.q: Which is the highest civilian honour in India?
NTPC.solution: Bharat Ratna is India's highest civilian honour, instituted in 1954.

NTPC.q: The 'White Revolution' in India is associated with:
NTPC.solution: White Revolution (Operation Flood) refers to the dairy development programme led by Dr. Verghese Kurien through NDDB/Amul.

NTPC.q: The 'Indian Ocean Dipole' affects:
options: Monsoon patterns in India and East Africa
NTPC.solution: The Indian Ocean Dipole (IOD) affects monsoon rainfall in India, East Africa, and Australia.

NTPC.q: Which article of the Indian Constitution deals with the Emergency provisions?
NTPC.solution: Emergency provisions are in Articles 352–360 (Part XVIII) of the Constitution — National, State, and Financial Emergencies.

NTPC.q: The Konkan Railway connects:
NTPC.solution: Konkan Railway runs 741 km from Roha (Maharashtra) to Mangaluru (Karnataka) along the western coast.

NTPC.q: The 'Panchtantra' stories were written by:
NTPC.solution: Panchtantra, the collection of animal fables, was composed by Vishnu Sharma around 300 BCE.

NTPC.q: Who established the 'Asiatic Society of Bengal' in 1784?
NTPC.solution: Sir William Jones founded the Asiatic Society of Bengal in 1784, contributing to Oriental studies.

NTPC.q: The 'National Stock Exchange (NSE)' is located in:
NTPC.solution: The National Stock Exchange (NSE) is headquartered in Mumbai (Bandra-Kurla Complex), Maharashtra.

NTPC.q: The 'Fiscal Deficit' is the difference between:
options: Total expenditure and total revenue receipts + recoveries
NTPC.solution: Fiscal deficit = Total government expenditure − (Revenue receipts + Capital receipts excluding borrowings).

NTPC.q: The 'Rajiv Gandhi Khel Ratna Award' has been renamed as:
options: Major Dhyan Chand Khel Ratna Award
NTPC.solution: In August 2021, the Rajiv Gandhi Khel Ratna was renamed Major Dhyan Chand Khel Ratna Award.

NTPC.q: Which river is known as the 'Lifeline of Rajasthan'?
NTPC.solution: The Indira Gandhi Canal (drawing from Sutlej-Beas) is called the Lifeline of Rajasthan as it irrigates the arid western region.

NTPC.q: The 'State List' in the Indian Constitution contains subjects on which:
options: Both Parliament and States can legislate
NTPC.solution: The State List (List II, Seventh Schedule) contains subjects on which only state legislatures can make laws.

NTPC.q: The 'Great Barrier Reef' is located in:
NTPC.solution: The Great Barrier Reef, the world's largest coral reef system, is located in the Coral Sea off Queensland, Australia.

NTPC.q: The 'Palk Strait' separates India from:
NTPC.solution: The Palk Strait is a narrow stretch of water separating Tamil Nadu (India) from Sri Lanka.

NTPC.q: The 'Finance Commission' of India is constituted every:
NTPC.solution: Under Article 280, the Finance Commission is constituted every 5 years to recommend revenue distribution between Centre and States.

NTPC.q: The 'Satpura Range' lies between which two rivers?
NTPC.solution: The Satpura Range lies between the Narmada (north) and the Tapi (south) rivers in central India.

NTPC.q: The 'Directive Principles of State Policy' were borrowed from the Constitution of:
NTPC.solution: The Directive Principles of State Policy were borrowed from the Irish Constitution of 1937.

NTPC.q: The 'Cripps Mission' of 1942 failed because:
options: It rejected Congress demand for immediate transfer of power and promised only Dominion Status after war
NTPC.hint: Gandhi called it a post-dated cheque.
NTPC.solution: The Cripps Mission failed as it offered only Dominion Status after WWII, not immediate self-governance — rejected by Congress.

NTPC.q: The concept of 'Judicial Review' in India means:
NTPC.solution: Judicial Review allows Indian courts to examine laws and executive actions and declare them void if they violate the Constitution.

NTPC.q: Which Act first introduced 'direct elections' to the Indian Legislative Council?
NTPC.solution: The Indian Councils Act 1909 (Morley-Minto Reforms) introduced direct elections to legislative councils for the first time.

NTPC.q: The 'Seventh Schedule' of the Constitution divides subjects into:
NTPC.solution: Seventh Schedule divides legislative subjects into Union List (97 subjects), State List (66 subjects), and Concurrent List (47 subjects).

NTPC.q: The 'Hunter Commission' (1882) was related to:
NTPC.solution: The Hunter Commission (1882) reviewed Indian education, leading to the expansion of primary and secondary education.

NTPC.q: The 'Permanent Settlement' of 1793 was introduced by:
NTPC.solution: Lord Cornwallis introduced the Permanent Settlement in Bengal in 1793, fixing land revenue permanently with zamindars.

NTPC.q: The 'Rowlatt Committee' was constituted to investigate:
NTPC.solution: The Rowlatt Committee (1918) investigated seditious movements and recommended repressive measures leading to the Rowlatt Act.

NTPC.q: Which Mughal emperor was known as 'Zinda Pir' (Living Saint)?
NTPC.solution: Aurangzeb was called Zinda Pir (Living Saint) for his austere, deeply religious Islamic lifestyle.

NTPC.q: The 'Suez Canal' connects:
NTPC.solution: The Suez Canal connects the Mediterranean Sea (at Port Said) to the Red Sea (at Suez), Egypt.

NTPC.q: Under the Indian Constitution, who can remove the 'Comptroller and Auditor General' from office?
options: President on address by both Houses of Parliament
NTPC.hint: Same procedure as removing a judge.
NTPC.solution: The CAG can be removed by the President only on an address passed by both Houses of Parliament, similar to the removal of a Supreme Court judge.

NTPC.q: The 'Second Battle of Panipat' (1556) was fought between:
NTPC.solution: The Second Battle of Panipat (1556) was fought between Akbar's forces (led by Bairam Khan) and Hemu, resulting in Mughal victory.

NTPC.q: The 'International Monetary Fund (IMF)' was established after:
NTPC.solution: IMF was established at the Bretton Woods Conference in 1944 to promote international monetary cooperation.

NTPC.q: The 'Mahalwari System' of land revenue was introduced by:
NTPC.solution: The Mahalwari System was introduced by Holt Mackenzie in the North-West Provinces, where revenue was settled with the village community.

NTPC.q: The 'Poona Pact' (1932) was between:
NTPC.solution: The Poona Pact (1932) between Gandhi and Ambedkar replaced separate electorates for Depressed Classes with reserved seats in general constituencies.

NTPC.q: The 'Mandal Commission' (1980) report recommended reservation for:
NTPC.solution: The Mandal Commission recommended 27% reservation in government jobs for OBCs, implemented in 1990 under V.P. Singh.

NTPC.q: India's 'Look East Policy' was launched by PM:
NTPC.solution: P.V. Narasimha Rao launched the Look East Policy in 1991 to strengthen ties with Southeast Asian countries.

NTPC.q: The 'National Development Council' of India is chaired by:
NTPC.solution: The National Development Council (NDC) is chaired by the Prime Minister and includes all state Chief Ministers.

NTPC.q: The 'Gandhian Principles' in the Indian Constitution are enshrined mainly in:
options: Directive Principles — rural development, cottage industries, prohibition
NTPC.solution: Gandhian principles like Panchayati Raj, cottage industries, prohibition, and cow protection are in the Directive Principles (DPSP).

NTPC.q: The 'Vernacular Press Act' of 1878 was repealed by:
NTPC.solution: Lord Ripon repealed the Vernacular Press Act in 1882, earning praise from Indians for his liberal policies.

NTPC.q: The 'Constituent Assembly' adopted the Indian Constitution on:
NTPC.solution: The Constituent Assembly adopted the Constitution on 26 November 1949 (now celebrated as Constitution Day).

NTPC.q: The 'Integrated Guided Missile Development Programme (IGMDP)' was headed by:
NTPC.solution: Dr. A.P.J. Abdul Kalam headed the IGMDP, developing missiles like Agni, Prithvi, Akash, Trishul, and Nag.

NTPC.q: The 'National Water Policy' of India aims to give priority to water use in the order:
NTPC.solution: The National Water Policy prioritises drinking water first, followed by agriculture, then industry and hydropower.

NTPC.q: The 'Vienna Convention on Diplomatic Relations' was adopted in:
NTPC.solution: The Vienna Convention on Diplomatic Relations was adopted in 1961 and codifies the rules for diplomatic immunity.

NTPC.q: The principle of 'One State, One Tax' (GST) replaced how many indirect taxes in India?
NTPC.hint: Multiple central and state taxes merged.
NTPC.solution: GST (implemented July 2017) subsumed 17+ central and state indirect taxes including VAT, service tax, and excise duty.

NTPC.q: The 'Doklam Standoff' of 2017 involved a dispute between India, China, and:
NTPC.solution: The Doklam standoff (2017) involved India backing Bhutan against China's road construction in disputed Doklam territory.

NTPC.q: The 'National Food Security Act' of India is aimed at providing subsidised food grain to approximately:
NTPC.solution: The National Food Security Act (2013) covers up to 67% of India's population — 75% rural and 50% urban.

NTPC.q: The 'Bharat Stage (BS) VI' emission norms were implemented in India from:
NTPC.solution: India skipped BS-V and directly moved to BS-VI emission norms from 1 April 2020.

NTPC.q: The headquarters of 'Reserve Bank of India' is located in:
NTPC.solution: RBI's headquarters is in Mumbai. It was originally established in Kolkata in 1935 but shifted to Mumbai in 1937.

NTPC.q: The 'Preamble' of the Indian Constitution was amended by which amendment?
NTPC.solution: The 42nd Constitutional Amendment (1976) added the words 'socialist', 'secular', and 'integrity' to the Preamble.

NTPC.q: The 'Indus Valley Civilisation' was primarily a:
NTPC.solution: The Indus Valley (Harappan) Civilisation (3300–1300 BCE) is known for its well-planned urban cities with drainage systems.

NTPC.q: Which article of the Constitution allows the Parliament to form new states?
NTPC.solution: Article 3 empowers Parliament to form new states, alter boundaries, names, and areas of existing states.

NTPC.q: The 'Tropic of Cancer' passes through how many Indian states?
NTPC.solution: The Tropic of Cancer (23.5°N) passes through 8 Indian states: Gujarat, Rajasthan, MP, Chhattisgarh, Jharkhand, West Bengal, Tripura, Mizoram.

NTPC.q: The 'National Pension System (NPS)' was introduced for central government employees from:
NTPC.solution: NPS was made mandatory for all central government employees joining service from 1 January 2004.

NTPC.q: The 'Nobel Prize' was established by the will of:
NTPC.solution: Alfred Nobel, the Swedish inventor of dynamite, established the Nobel Prize in his 1895 will.

NTPC.q: Which is the deepest ocean in the world?
NTPC.solution: The Pacific Ocean is the world's deepest ocean, containing the Mariana Trench (deepest point ~11 km).

NTPC.q: The 'Ryotwari System' of land revenue was introduced in:
NTPC.solution: The Ryotwari system, introduced by Thomas Munro in Madras and Bombay Presidencies, settled revenue directly with individual cultivators.

NTPC.q: The 'Anti-Defection Law' is contained in which schedule of the Constitution?
NTPC.solution: The Tenth Schedule (added by 52nd Amendment, 1985) contains the anti-defection law, disqualifying legislators who defect from their party.

NTPC.q: The 'National Green Tribunal (NGT)' was established under:
NTPC.solution: NGT was established under the National Green Tribunal Act 2010 for speedy environmental dispute resolution.

NTPC.q: The 'Silk Road' ancient trade route connected China to:
options: Europe and Western Asia via Central Asia
NTPC.solution: The Silk Road connected China to the Mediterranean world through Central Asia, Persia, and the Middle East, enabling trade in silk, spices, and other goods.

NTPC.q: Which country is the world's largest producer of crude oil (2023)?
NTPC.solution: The USA has been the world's largest crude oil producer since around 2018, driven by the shale oil revolution.

NTPC.q: The 'Collegium System' for appointment of judges in India was established by:
options: Supreme Court in the Second Judges Case (1993)
NTPC.solution: The Collegium System for appointment and transfer of judges was evolved by the Supreme Court in the Second Judges Case (1993).

NTPC.q: The 'Pradhan Mantri Fasal Bima Yojana' was launched in:
NTPC.solution: Pradhan Mantri Fasal Bima Yojana (PMFBY), a crop insurance scheme for farmers, was launched in January 2016.

NTPC.q: The 'Eastern Ghats' run parallel to which coast of India?
NTPC.solution: The Eastern Ghats run parallel to the eastern coast of India, along the Bay of Bengal.

NTPC.q: The 'Himalayas' are an example of which type of mountain?
NTPC.solution: The Himalayas are fold mountains, formed by the collision of the Indian and Eurasian tectonic plates.

NTPC.q: The 'Chipko Movement' of 1973 was primarily a protest against:
NTPC.solution: The Chipko Movement in Chamoli, Uttarakhand was a grassroots protest against commercial felling of forest trees.

NTPC.q: The 'Narasimham Committee' (1991 and 1998) was related to:
NTPC.solution: The Narasimham Committee reports (1991 and 1998) recommended major banking and financial sector reforms in India.

NTPC.q: The 'Right to Information Act' was passed in India in:
NTPC.hint: Magsaysay Award winner Aruna Roy championed it.
NTPC.solution: The Right to Information Act was passed in June 2005, giving citizens the right to access government information.

NTPC.q: The 'Beti Bachao Beti Padhao' scheme was launched to address:
options: Female foeticide and girl child education
NTPC.solution: Beti Bachao Beti Padhao was launched in 2015 to address declining child sex ratio and promote education of the girl child.

NTPC.q: The 'Quit India Resolution' was passed at which session of Congress?
NTPC.solution: The Quit India Resolution was passed on 8 August 1942 at the Bombay session of Congress (Gowalia Tank Maidan), followed by Gandhi's 'Do or Die' call.

NTPC.q: The 'Western Disturbances' bring rainfall to which region of India?
options: Northwest India (Punjab, Haryana, Himachal Pradesh) in winter
NTPC.solution: Western Disturbances are extratropical cyclones that originate in the Mediterranean and bring winter rainfall to northwest India.

NTPC.q: The 'Delimitation Commission' in India is constituted under:
NTPC.solution: The Delimitation Commission is constituted under Article 82 of the Constitution read with the Delimitation Commission Acts.

NTPC.q: The 'Veto Power' in the United Nations Security Council is held by:
NTPC.solution: The five permanent members (P5) — USA, UK, France, Russia, and China — hold veto power in the UN Security Council.

NTPC.q: The 'Brahmaputra' river originates from:
NTPC.solution: The Brahmaputra originates from the Chemayungdung Glacier near Mansarovar (Tibet) and is known as Yarlung Tsangpo there.

NTPC.q: The 'National Commission for Women' was established in:
NTPC.solution: The National Commission for Women was established in January 1992 under the NCW Act, 1990.

NTPC.q: The 'Bonn Convention' is related to:
NTPC.solution: The Bonn Convention (Convention on Migratory Species, 1979) aims to conserve terrestrial, marine, and avian migratory species.

NTPC.q: India's first Five-Year Plan gave highest priority to:
NTPC.solution: India's First Five-Year Plan (1951–56) gave highest priority to agriculture to address post-partition food shortage.

NTPC.q: The term 'Prorogation' of Parliament means:
options: Termination of a session without dissolving the House
NTPC.solution: Prorogation terminates a session of Parliament without dissolving it; the Lok Sabha continues but no new business can be transacted until the next session.

NTPC.q: The 'Clean Ganga Mission (Namami Gange)' was launched in:
NTPC.solution: Namami Gange was launched in 2014 with a budget of ₹20,000 crore to clean and rejuvenate the Ganga river.

NTPC.q: The 'Karachi Session' of INC (1931) adopted a resolution on:
NTPC.solution: The Karachi Session of Congress (1931) adopted a landmark resolution on Fundamental Rights and National Economic Policy drafted by Nehru.

NTPC.q: The 'Bharat Ratna' is awarded for:
options: Exceptional service of the highest order in any field of human endeavour
NTPC.solution: Bharat Ratna is awarded for the highest order of service to the nation in any field, including arts, science, literature, and public service.

NTPC.q: The 'Third Battle of Panipat' (1761) was fought between:
NTPC.solution: The Third Battle of Panipat (1761) between the Marathas and Ahmad Shah Abdali (Afghan) resulted in a catastrophic Maratha defeat.

NTPC.q: The 'Subrahmanyam Bharati' is associated with which Indian language literary movement?
NTPC.solution: Subrahmanyam Bharati (1882–1921) was a Tamil poet, writer, and nationalist who played a key role in Tamil literary renaissance.

NTPC.q: The 'Sunderbans' is famous for which animal?
NTPC.solution: The Sundarbans in West Bengal and Bangladesh is the largest mangrove forest and home to the Royal Bengal Tiger.

NTPC.q: The 'Centre-State relations' in India follow which model?
NTPC.hint: Dr. Ambedkar called it a 'union of states'.
NTPC.solution: India is quasi-federal — it has federal features but with a strong centre. Dr. Ambedkar called it a 'Union of States'.

NTPC.q: The 'Instrument of Accession' by which Indian princely states joined India was signed under which framework?
NTPC.solution: The Indian Independence Act 1947 provided the legal framework for Instruments of Accession, through which princely states joined India or Pakistan.

NTPC.q: The 'Rajasthan Canal' (Indira Gandhi Canal) is fed by which dam?
NTPC.solution: The Indira Gandhi Canal begins at Harike Barrage at the confluence of the Sutlej and Beas rivers in Punjab.

NTPC.q: Which case established that the right to 'privacy' is a Fundamental Right in India?
NTPC.solution: In K.S. Puttaswamy v. Union of India (2017), a nine-judge bench of the Supreme Court unanimously held that the right to privacy is a Fundamental Right under Article 21.

NTPC.q: The 'Swachh Bharat Mission' was launched on:
NTPC.solution: Swachh Bharat Mission was launched on 2 October 2014 (Gandhi Jayanti) to achieve Open Defecation Free India.

NTPC.q: The 'Pradhan Mantri Jan Dhan Yojana (PMJDY)' focuses on:
options: Financial inclusion — opening bank accounts for unbanked population
NTPC.solution: PMJDY (launched 2014) aims for financial inclusion by providing a bank account, RuPay debit card, and insurance to every unbanked household.

NTPC.q: The 'Andaman and Nicobar Islands' are separated from Myanmar's Coco Islands by:
NTPC.solution: The Coco Channel separates the Andaman and Nicobar Islands of India from the Coco Islands (Myanmar).

NTPC.q: The 'UDAN (Ude Desh Ka Aam Nagrik)' scheme is related to:
options: Regional air connectivity for smaller cities
NTPC.solution: UDAN is a regional connectivity scheme launched in 2016 to make air travel affordable for common citizens in tier-2 and tier-3 cities.

NTPC.q: Who wrote the book 'Discovery of India'?
NTPC.solution: Jawaharlal Nehru wrote 'The Discovery of India' during his imprisonment in Ahmednagar Fort (1944–46).

ALP.q: What is the SI unit of Resistance?
ALP.hint: Represented by the Greek symbol Omega.
ALP.solution: Ohm is the standard unit of electrical resistance.

ALP.q: What is the average normal human body temperature?
ALP.solution: 37°C is the standard average temperature.

ALP.q: Which instrument is used to measure electric current?
ALP.solution: An Ammeter measures the flow of current in Amperes.

ALP.q: The process of converting solid directly into gas is called:
ALP.hint: Think of dry ice or camphor.
ALP.solution: Sublimation is the transition without passing through the liquid phase.

ALP.q: If the length of a wire is doubled, its resistance will:
ALP.hint: Resistance is directly proportional to length.
ALP.solution: R = ρ(L/A); doubling length doubles resistance.

ALP.q: Which part of the brain controls voluntary actions and balance?
ALP.solution: The cerebellum coordinates muscle movements and balance.

ALP.q: What is the frequency of AC supply in India?
ALP.solution: India uses a 50 Hz frequency for its power grid.

ALP.q: Which mirror is used as a rear-view mirror in vehicles?
ALP.hint: It provides a wider field of view.
ALP.solution: Convex mirrors form diminished, upright images over a large area.

ALP.q: Sound waves in air are always:
ALP.hint: Particles move parallel to the wave.
ALP.solution: Sound waves are longitudinal mechanical waves.

ALP.q: The escape velocity from Earth's surface is approximately:
ALP.solution: It is roughly 11.2 km per second.

ALP.q: The working principle of an optical fiber is:
ALP.solution: Total Internal Reflection (TIR) allows light to travel with minimal loss.

ALP.q: Which of the following is a primary color of light?
ALP.solution: The primary colors of light are Red, Green, and Blue.

ALP.q: A fuse wire is characterized by:
ALP.hint: It must melt easily during an overload.
ALP.solution: A fuse wire needs high resistance to heat up and a low melting point to break the circuit.

ALP.q: What is the commercial unit of electrical energy?
ALP.solution: 1 kWh (Kilowatt-hour) is the standard commercial unit of energy.

ALP.q: In an electric motor, the direction of rotation is determined by:
ALP.solution: Fleming's Left Hand Rule is used for motors; Right Hand for Generators.

ALP.q: What is the SI unit of force?
ALP.solution: Newton (N) is the SI unit of force, named after Isaac Newton.

ALP.q: Which gas is produced during photosynthesis?
ALP.solution: Oxygen (O2) is released during photosynthesis as a byproduct.

ALP.q: What is the chemical formula of water?
ALP.solution: Water has the formula H2O — two hydrogen atoms bonded to one oxygen.

ALP.q: The unit of electric charge is:
ALP.solution: Coulomb (C) is the SI unit of electric charge.

ALP.q: Which colour has the longest wavelength in the visible spectrum?
ALP.solution: Red has the longest wavelength (~700 nm) in the visible spectrum.

ALP.q: A concave lens always produces which type of image?
ALP.solution: A concave (diverging) lens always produces virtual, erect, and diminished images.

ALP.q: What is the boiling point of water at standard pressure?
ALP.solution: Water boils at 100°C (212°F) at standard atmospheric pressure (1 atm).

ALP.q: Newton's first law of motion is also called:
ALP.solution: Newton's 1st Law is the Law of Inertia — body stays at rest or in motion unless acted on.

ALP.q: The density of water is:
ALP.solution: Water has a density of 1.0 g/cm³ (1000 kg/m³) at 4°C.

ALP.q: Which type of circuit has the same current through all components?
ALP.solution: In a series circuit, the same current flows through all components.

ALP.q: The focal length of a convex lens is:
ALP.solution: Convex (converging) lenses have positive focal lengths by sign convention.

ALP.q: Photons are associated with which force?
ALP.solution: Photons are the force carriers of the electromagnetic force.

ALP.q: What does LED stand for?
ALP.solution: LED stands for Light Emitting Diode, which emits light when current passes through it.

ALP.q: The power of a lens is measured in:
ALP.solution: Power of a lens is measured in Dioptres (D), equal to reciprocal of focal length in metres.

ALP.q: In which medium does light travel fastest?
ALP.solution: Light travels fastest in vacuum at approximately 3×10⁸ m/s.

ALP.q: Electrical energy is converted into mechanical energy in a:
ALP.solution: An electric motor converts electrical energy into mechanical (rotational) energy.

ALP.q: The human eye can adjust its focal length using:
ALP.solution: Ciliary muscles change the curvature of the lens to adjust focal length.

ALP.q: Which type of mirror is used in solar furnaces?
ALP.solution: Concave mirrors converge sunlight at focal point to produce intense heat.

ALP.q: What is the unit of frequency?
ALP.solution: Hertz (Hz) is the SI unit of frequency — one cycle per second.

ALP.q: The transformer works on the principle of:
ALP.solution: Transformers work on mutual electromagnetic induction between two coils.

ALP.q: When a body is in free fall, its acceleration equals:
ALP.solution: In free fall, acceleration = g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (ignoring air resistance).

ALP.q: Electromagnetic induction was discovered by:
ALP.solution: Michael Faraday discovered electromagnetic induction in 1831.

ALP.q: The persistence of vision is approximately:
ALP.solution: The retina retains an image for about 1/16th of a second — the basis of movies.

ALP.q: The phenomenon of bending of light around sharp corners is called:
ALP.solution: Diffraction is the bending of waves around edges or through small openings.

ALP.q: In a step-up transformer, the secondary voltage is:
ALP.solution: Step-up transformers have more turns in secondary coil, increasing output voltage.

ALP.q: What is the atomic number of hydrogen?
ALP.solution: Hydrogen has atomic number 1 — one proton in its nucleus.

ALP.q: Which lens is used to correct myopia (short-sightedness)?
ALP.solution: Concave (diverging) lenses are used to correct myopia by diverging light rays.

ALP.q: Ohm's law is not applicable to:
ALP.solution: Semiconductor diodes are non-ohmic — they don't follow Ohm's Law linearly.

ALP.q: If resistance is halved and voltage is doubled, current becomes:
ALP.solution: I = V/R. If V doubles and R halves: I = 2V/(R/2) = 4V/R = 4× original.

ALP.q: Sound cannot travel through:
ALP.solution: Sound is a mechanical wave and cannot propagate through vacuum.

ALP.q: Which property of sound determines its pitch?
ALP.solution: Pitch is determined by frequency — higher frequency gives higher pitch.

ALP.q: The phenomenon of total internal reflection is used in:
ALP.solution: Optical fibres use TIR to transmit light signals with minimal loss.

ALP.q: The efficiency of a machine is always:
ALP.solution: Efficiency < 100% due to friction, heat loss, and other energy dissipation.

ALP.q: The phenomenon of light splitting into colours by a prism is called:
ALP.solution: Dispersion splits white light into its constituent colours (VIBGYOR).

ALP.q: Lenz's Law is a consequence of conservation of:
ALP.solution: Lenz's Law is a consequence of conservation of energy — induced EMF opposes change.

ALP.q: Which particle has negative charge?
ALP.solution: Electrons carry a negative charge of 1.6×10⁻¹⁹ coulombs.

ALP.q: Work done is zero when force and displacement are:
ALP.solution: W = Fd cosθ; when θ=90°, cos90°=0, so W=0.

ALP.q: A dynamo (generator) converts:
ALP.solution: A generator/dynamo converts mechanical energy into electrical energy.

ALP.q: The specific heat capacity of water is:
ALP.solution: Water's specific heat capacity is 4.2 J/g°C, the highest of common substances.

ALP.q: The ratio of charge to mass of an electron is approximately:
ALP.solution: The charge-to-mass ratio (e/m) of electron is 1.76×10¹¹ C/kg.

ALP.q: Which gas is used in fluorescent lamps?
ALP.solution: Mercury vapour produces UV radiation inside fluorescent lamps, which excites the phosphor coating.

ALP.q: The phenomenon of apparent bending of light at medium boundary is:
ALP.solution: Refraction is the bending of light when it passes from one medium to another.

ALP.q: Kinetic energy depends on:
ALP.solution: Kinetic energy = ½mv², depending on both mass and the square of velocity.

ALP.q: An object placed at the centre of curvature of a concave mirror forms image:
ALP.solution: Object at C produces real, inverted, same-size image also at C.

ALP.q: The principle of 'superposition of waves' states:
options: Net displacement equals sum of individual displacements
ALP.solution: Superposition principle: net displacement at any point equals the sum of all wave displacements.

ALP.q: What is the unit of magnetic flux?
ALP.solution: Weber (Wb) is the SI unit of magnetic flux. 1 Wb = 1 V·s.

ALP.q: In a nuclear fission reaction, which particle splits the nucleus?
ALP.hint: No charge, so it can penetrate.
ALP.solution: Slow neutrons are used to initiate fission in uranium-235.

ALP.q: The critical angle for total internal reflection depends on:
ALP.solution: The critical angle depends on the refractive index: sin(c) = 1/n.

ALP.q: A wire that resists flow of electric current is called:
ALP.solution: Insulators resist the flow of electric current. Examples: rubber, wood, glass.

ALP.q: What type of radiation has the highest penetrating power?
ALP.solution: Gamma rays have the highest penetrating power among nuclear radiations.

ALP.q: Eddy currents are produced in:
options: Bulk conductors in changing magnetic fields
ALP.solution: Eddy currents are induced in bulk conductors by changing magnetic fields.

ALP.q: The loudness of sound depends on:
ALP.solution: Loudness depends on the amplitude of sound waves — larger amplitude = louder sound.

ALP.q: Which mirror has a reflecting surface curved inward?
ALP.solution: Concave mirror has an inward curved reflecting surface and converges light.

ALP.q: A thermistor is a device whose resistance changes with:
ALP.solution: A thermistor is a thermally sensitive resistor — resistance varies with temperature.

ALP.q: The process of gaining electrons is called:
ALP.solution: Reduction involves gaining electrons (Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain).

ALP.q: The focal length of a plane mirror is:
ALP.solution: A plane mirror has infinite focal length as it neither converges nor diverges light.

ALP.q: Which quantity remains conserved in elastic collisions?
ALP.solution: In elastic collisions both kinetic energy and momentum are conserved.

ALP.q: The Hall effect demonstrates the presence of:
ALP.solution: The Hall effect proves the existence and type of charge carriers in a conductor.

ALP.q: Which device is used to measure temperature?
ALP.solution: A thermometer measures temperature. Clinical thermometers measure body temperature.

ALP.q: The speed of sound is greatest in:
ALP.solution: Sound travels fastest in steel (~5,960 m/s) compared to water (~1,500 m/s) or air (~340 m/s).

ALP.q: The photoelectric effect was explained by:
ALP.solution: Einstein explained the photoelectric effect using photon concept, winning the 1921 Nobel Prize.

ALP.q: The unit of power is:
ALP.solution: Watt (W) is the SI unit of power. 1 Watt = 1 Joule per second.

ALP.q: What is the function of a capacitor in an AC circuit?
ALP.solution: Capacitors block DC and allow AC to pass through.

ALP.q: The resonant frequency in an LC circuit is:
ALP.solution: Resonant frequency f = 1/(2π√(LC)) where L is inductance and C is capacitance.

ALP.q: How many colours are there in white light (rainbow)?
ALP.solution: White light splits into 7 colours: Violet, Indigo, Blue, Green, Yellow, Orange, Red.

ALP.q: The device used to convert AC to DC is called:
ALP.solution: A rectifier converts alternating current (AC) to direct current (DC).

ALP.q: Which law relates the voltage across an inductor to current change?
ALP.solution: V = L(dI/dt) — voltage across inductor equals inductance × rate of current change.

ALP.q: Which of these is a renewable source of energy?
ALP.solution: Solar energy is renewable as it comes from the sun which is virtually inexhaustible.

ALP.q: The pressure exerted by a liquid at depth h is:
ALP.solution: Pressure at depth h in a liquid: P = ρgh, where ρ is density and g is gravity.

ALP.q: The stopping potential in photoelectric effect depends on:
ALP.solution: Stopping potential depends on frequency of incident light, not intensity.

ALP.q: The angle of incidence equals the angle of reflection in:
ALP.solution: The law of reflection states angle of incidence = angle of reflection.

ALP.q: A charged particle moving in a magnetic field experiences:
ALP.solution: A moving charge in a magnetic field experiences Lorentz/magnetic force: F = qvB sinθ.

ALP.q: Which type of energy does a compressed spring possess?
ALP.solution: A compressed spring stores elastic potential energy.

ALP.q: What happens to resistance when temperature of a metal increases?
ALP.solution: For metals, resistance increases with temperature due to increased atomic vibrations.

ALP.q: In an n-p-n transistor, the base is made of:
ALP.solution: In n-p-n transistor, the base is p-type, sandwiched between two n-type regions.

ALP.q: The unit of magnetic field strength is:
ALP.solution: Tesla (T) is the SI unit of magnetic field strength (magnetic flux density).

ALP.q: Which is the largest planet in our solar system?
ALP.solution: Jupiter is the largest planet in our solar system, with mass 2.5× all other planets combined.

ALP.q: Wien's displacement law is related to:
ALP.solution: Wien's displacement law states that peak wavelength of blackbody radiation × temperature = constant.

ALP.q: A fuse is connected in:
ALP.solution: A fuse is always connected in series so the full current passes through it.

ALP.q: The phenomenon of emission of electrons from a metal on heating is:
ALP.solution: Thermionic emission is the release of electrons from heated metal surfaces.

ALP.q: What does AM in AM radio stand for?
ALP.solution: AM stands for Amplitude Modulation — the audio signal modulates carrier wave amplitude.

ALP.q: Which force keeps planets in orbit around the Sun?
ALP.solution: Gravitational force provides the centripetal force keeping planets in orbit.

ALP.q: The working of a microphone is based on:
ALP.solution: A microphone converts sound waves into electrical signals using electromagnetic induction.

ALP.q: De Broglie's hypothesis states every moving particle has:
ALP.solution: De Broglie proposed every moving particle has an associated wavelength: λ = h/mv.

ALP.q: Which organ in the human body is responsible for pumping blood?
ALP.solution: The heart pumps blood throughout the body. It beats ~72 times/min at rest.

ALP.q: The number of electrons in the outermost shell of carbon is:
ALP.solution: Carbon has 4 electrons in its outermost shell (2,4 configuration), giving it valency 4.

ALP.q: The time constant of an RC circuit is:
ALP.solution: Time constant τ = RC. It is the time for capacitor to charge to ~63.2% of supply voltage.

ALP.q: Which phenomenon explains why the sky is blue?
ALP.solution: Blue light scatters more than red (Rayleigh scattering), making the sky appear blue.

ALP.q: LASER stands for:
options: Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission of Radiation
Light Application for Stimulated Energy Release
Luminous Amplification by Special Energy Release
ALP.solution: LASER = Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission of Radiation.

ALP.q: The SI unit of temperature is:
ALP.solution: Kelvin (K) is the SI unit of temperature. 0°C = 273.15 K.

ALP.q: A step-down transformer is used to:
ALP.solution: Step-down transformer reduces voltage by having fewer secondary turns than primary.

ALP.q: Kirchhoff's voltage law (KVL) is based on conservation of:
ALP.hint: Sum of EMFs = sum of voltage drops.
ALP.solution: KVL states sum of all voltages in a closed loop = 0, based on energy conservation.

ALP.q: What is the main constituent of natural gas?
ALP.solution: Methane (CH₄) is the primary constituent of natural gas (70–90%).

ALP.q: Which disease is caused by Vitamin C deficiency?
ALP.solution: Scurvy is caused by Vitamin C (ascorbic acid) deficiency.

ALP.q: The instrument used to measure atmospheric pressure is:
ALP.solution: A barometer measures atmospheric pressure, invented by Evangelista Torricelli in 1643.

ALP.q: The bandwidth of a signal is:
ALP.hint: Difference of high and low frequency.
ALP.solution: Bandwidth = difference between highest and lowest frequencies in the signal.

ALP.q: The resistance of a conductor is directly proportional to its:
ALP.solution: R ∝ L (length). Longer wire = greater resistance at constant cross-section.

ALP.q: Doppler effect is observed when:
options: Source and observer have relative motion
ALP.solution: Doppler effect: apparent change in frequency when source and observer have relative motion.

ALP.q: Which is the hardest natural substance on Earth?
ALP.solution: Diamond is the hardest natural substance, rating 10 on the Mohs hardness scale.

ALP.q: In a common emitter transistor amplifier, the output is taken from:
ALP.solution: In common emitter configuration, output is taken from the collector terminal.

ALP.q: What is the SI unit of electric resistance?
ALP.solution: Ohm (Ω) is the SI unit of electrical resistance, named after Georg Simon Ohm.

ALP.q: Which force acts between two charged particles?
ALP.solution: Electrostatic (Coulomb) force acts between charged particles — attractive or repulsive.

ALP.q: The process of heat transfer through direct contact is called:
ALP.solution: Conduction transfers heat through direct contact between particles of a material.

ALP.q: What is the unit of work done?
ALP.solution: Joule (J) is the unit of work. 1 J = 1 N × 1 m.

ALP.q: Which type of energy is stored in a battery?
ALP.solution: A battery stores chemical energy and converts it to electrical energy during use.

ALP.q: The angle of incidence is measured from the:
ALP.solution: The angle of incidence is measured from the normal (perpendicular) to the reflecting surface.

ALP.q: Which device converts electrical energy into sound?
ALP.solution: A loudspeaker converts electrical energy into sound using electromagnetic induction.

ALP.q: The momentum of a body is defined as:
ALP.solution: Momentum p = m × v (mass × velocity). It is a vector quantity.

ALP.q: The phenomenon of interference of light proves that light is a:
ALP.solution: Interference (Young's experiment) demonstrates the wave nature of light.

ALP.q: In a nuclear reactor, the moderator is used to:
ALP.solution: The moderator (e.g., heavy water, graphite) slows neutrons to sustain chain reaction.

ALP.q: The refractive index of water with respect to air is approximately:
ALP.solution: The refractive index of water ≈ 1.33, meaning light slows down when entering water.

ALP.q: Which type of wave requires a medium to travel?
ALP.solution: Mechanical waves (e.g., sound, water waves) need a material medium to propagate.

ALP.q: Ohm's law states that V is proportional to I when:
ALP.solution: Ohm's law holds when temperature and other physical conditions remain constant.

ALP.q: The SI unit of luminous intensity is:
ALP.solution: Candela (cd) is the SI base unit of luminous intensity.

ALP.q: The nuclear force is:
ALP.solution: The nuclear (strong) force is extremely strong but acts over very short ranges (within nucleus).

ALP.q: Which mirror is used as a rear-view mirror in vehicles?
ALP.solution: Convex mirrors are used as rear-view mirrors because they give a wider field of view.

ALP.q: The unit of electrical energy consumed is:
ALP.solution: Kilowatt-hour (kWh) is the commercial unit of electrical energy. 1 kWh = 3.6 × 10⁶ J.

ALP.q: Newton's third law states:
options: For every action there is an equal and opposite reaction
ALP.solution: Newton's 3rd law: every action has an equal and opposite reaction.

ALP.q: The image formed by a plane mirror is:
ALP.hint: Cannot be obtained on a screen.
ALP.solution: A plane mirror forms a virtual, erect, and laterally inverted image of the same size.

ALP.q: The escape velocity from Earth's surface is approximately:
ALP.solution: Escape velocity from Earth is approximately 11.2 km/s — needed to escape Earth's gravity.

ALP.q: The principle of conservation of linear momentum applies when:
options: No external force acts on the system
ALP.solution: Linear momentum is conserved in a system when no external force acts on it (isolated system).

ALP.q: Chadwick discovered the neutron in:
ALP.solution: James Chadwick discovered the neutron in 1932, completing the nuclear model of the atom.

ALP.q: The phenomenon of polarisation confirms light is a:
ALP.hint: Only transverse waves can be polarised.
ALP.solution: Polarisation occurs only in transverse waves, confirming light is a transverse wave.

ALP.q: The temperature at which a material loses its electrical resistance completely is called:
ALP.solution: The critical temperature is the temperature below which a material becomes superconducting (zero resistance).

ALP.q: The half-life of a radioactive substance is the time in which:
ALP.solution: Half-life is the time in which half the atoms decay and the activity reduces to half.

ALP.q: What is the unit of frequency?
ALP.solution: Hertz (Hz) is the unit of frequency. 1 Hz = 1 cycle per second.

ALP.q: A body at rest will stay at rest unless:
options: An external force acts on it
ALP.solution: Newton's 1st Law (Law of Inertia): a body at rest stays at rest unless acted upon by an external force.

ALP.q: The phenomenon when light bends around obstacles is called:
ALP.solution: Diffraction is the bending of light (or any wave) around the edges of obstacles or slits.

ALP.q: Which device is used to step up or step down AC voltage?
ALP.solution: A transformer is used to step up or step down AC voltages using mutual electromagnetic induction.

ALP.q: The image formed by a concave mirror when object is between F and P is:
ALP.solution: When object is between focus (F) and pole (P) of a concave mirror, the image is virtual, erect, and magnified.

ALP.q: The force between two parallel wires carrying current in the same direction is:
ALP.solution: Two parallel wires carrying current in the same direction attract each other.

ALP.q: The magnetic susceptibility of a diamagnetic material is:
ALP.solution: Diamagnetic materials have small negative susceptibility — they are weakly repelled by magnets.

ALP.q: Bernoulli's principle states that in fluid flow, increased speed results in:
ALP.solution: Bernoulli's principle: as fluid speed increases, pressure decreases (conservation of energy).

ALP.q: Alternating current changes direction:
ALP.solution: AC reverses direction periodically. In India at 50 Hz, it changes direction 100 times per second.

ALP.q: The 'dioptre' is the unit of:
ALP.solution: Dioptre is the unit of power of a lens. Power = 1/focal length (in metres).

ALP.q: What is the chemical formula of rust?
ALP.solution: Rust is hydrated iron(III) oxide, Fe₂O₃·nH₂O, formed by iron reacting with oxygen and water.

ALP.q: The relationship between pressure and volume at constant temperature is:
ALP.solution: Boyle's Law: at constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (PV = k).

ALP.q: The phenomenon of total internal reflection requires light to travel from:
ALP.solution: TIR occurs when light travels from denser to rarer medium at an angle greater than the critical angle.

ALP.q: The work function of a metal is the:
options: Minimum energy to emit electrons from surface
ALP.solution: Work function is the minimum energy required to eject a photoelectron from a metal surface.

ALP.q: According to Bohr's model, electrons can only move in orbits where angular momentum is:
ALP.solution: Bohr's quantisation: angular momentum = n(h/2π) where n is a positive integer.

ALP.q: The ratio of output power to input power in a machine is called:
ALP.solution: Efficiency = (output power / input power) × 100%. Always < 100% due to losses.

ALP.q: Which colour of light has the maximum speed in glass?
ALP.solution: Red light travels fastest in glass because it has the lowest refractive index in glass.

ALP.q: The working principle of a sonar system is based on:
ALP.solution: SONAR works on the principle of echo (reflection) of ultrasonic sound waves underwater.

ALP.q: Pressure in a liquid increases with:
ALP.solution: Liquid pressure P = ρgh increases with depth h as more liquid weight acts from above.

ALP.q: The device used to measure the electromotive force (EMF) of a cell is:
ALP.solution: A potentiometer accurately measures EMF of a cell using the null deflection method.

ALP.q: The energy stored in an inductor is:
ALP.hint: Similar to ½mv² for kinetic energy.
ALP.solution: Energy stored in an inductor = ½LI² where L is inductance and I is current.

ALP.q: A rainbow is formed due to:
options: Dispersion and total internal reflection of sunlight in droplets
ALP.solution: Rainbows form due to dispersion (splitting) and total internal reflection of sunlight in water droplets.

ALP.q: The process of converting liquid into vapour by boiling is called:
ALP.solution: Vaporisation is the general term for liquid-to-vapour conversion, including both evaporation and boiling.

ALP.q: The first law of thermodynamics is essentially:
ALP.solution: First law of thermodynamics is conservation of energy: ΔU = Q − W (heat added minus work done).

ALP.q: The phenomenon of electromagnetic induction was discovered by Faraday in:
ALP.solution: Michael Faraday discovered electromagnetic induction in 1831 through his coil experiments.

ALP.q: Which wave property determines the energy of a photon?
ALP.solution: Photon energy E = hf (Planck's constant × frequency). Higher frequency = more energy.

ALP.q: A transistor can be used as an amplifier because:
options: Small base current controls large collector current
ALP.solution: In a transistor, a small base current controls a much larger collector current, enabling amplification.

ALP.q: The human ear can hear sound in the frequency range of:
ALP.solution: The human ear detects sounds between 20 Hz (infrasound) and 20,000 Hz (ultrasound) — audible range.

ALP.q: The Archimedes' principle states that the buoyant force equals:
ALP.solution: Archimedes' principle: buoyant force = weight of the fluid displaced by the object.

ALP.q: The uncertainty principle was proposed by:
ALP.solution: Werner Heisenberg proposed the Uncertainty Principle: Δx × Δp ≥ ℏ/2.

ALP.q: In a parallel connection of resistors, the voltage across each resistor is:
ALP.solution: In a parallel circuit, all resistors are connected across the same two points, so voltage is equal.

ALP.q: Sound waves are:
ALP.solution: Sound waves are longitudinal waves — particles oscillate parallel to the direction of wave propagation.

ALP.q: The energy gap in an insulator is approximately:
ALP.solution: Insulators have a large energy band gap (>5 eV) preventing electron flow.

ALP.q: The bending of light when it passes from air into glass is due to:
ALP.solution: Refraction occurs because light changes speed when passing between media of different optical densities.

ALP.q: Fleming's Left Hand Rule gives direction of:
ALP.solution: Fleming's Left Hand Rule gives the direction of force (motion) on a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field.

ALP.q: The gravitational force between two bodies depends on:
ALP.solution: Gravitational force F = Gm₁m₂/r² — depends on both masses and the square of the distance.

ALP.q: The unit of magnetic flux density (B field) is:
ALP.solution: Tesla (T) is the SI unit of magnetic flux density (B). 1 T = 1 Wb/m².

ALP.q: Convex lens is also known as:
ALP.solution: A convex (converging) lens refracts parallel rays to converge at the focal point.

ALP.q: The Stefan-Boltzmann law states that radiated power is proportional to:
ALP.solution: Stefan-Boltzmann law: P = σAT⁴. Radiated power is proportional to the 4th power of absolute temperature.

ALP.q: What does RMS stand for in AC circuits?
ALP.solution: RMS (Root Mean Square) is the effective value of AC. V_rms = V_peak/√2.

ALP.q: A solar cell converts:
ALP.solution: Solar cells work on the photovoltaic effect — converting light energy directly into electrical energy.

ALP.q: The wavelength of visible light ranges approximately from:
ALP.solution: Visible light ranges from approximately 400 nm (violet) to 700 nm (red).

ALP.q: In a series circuit, if one component fails (breaks), the circuit:
ALP.solution: In a series circuit, a single break stops current in the entire circuit.

ALP.q: The Compton effect demonstrates the:
ALP.solution: The Compton effect (X-ray scattering) confirms that photons have momentum, showing particle nature of light.

ALP.q: The device that works on the principle of piezoelectric effect is:
ALP.solution: Crystal oscillators and ultrasound transducers work on the piezoelectric effect (pressure produces EMF).

ALP.q: How many colours are there in a rainbow?
ALP.solution: A rainbow has 7 colours: Violet, Indigo, Blue, Green, Yellow, Orange, Red (VIBGYOR).

ALP.q: The image formed in a pinhole camera is:
ALP.solution: A pinhole camera forms a real, inverted image because light travels in straight lines.

ALP.q: In a p-type semiconductor, the majority charge carriers are:
ALP.solution: In p-type semiconductor, holes are the majority carriers created by acceptor impurity atoms.

ALP.q: Pressure = Force / Area. If area doubles and force stays same, pressure:
ALP.solution: P = F/A. If area doubles, pressure halves (inversely proportional).

ALP.q: The speed of electromagnetic waves in vacuum is approximately:
ALP.solution: All electromagnetic waves travel at c ≈ 3 × 10⁸ m/s in vacuum.

ALP.q: Which process produces heat in a resistance due to current flow?
ALP.solution: Joule heating (I²R loss) produces heat in a resistor when current flows through it.

ALP.q: The charge on one electron is approximately:
ALP.solution: The charge of one electron e = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ Coulombs (negative for electron).

ALP.q: The specific gravity of a substance is its density relative to:
ALP.solution: Specific gravity (relative density) = density of substance / density of water at 4°C.

ALP.q: When two equal and opposite forces act on a body at different points, they form a:
ALP.solution: Two equal and opposite forces acting at different points form a couple, creating a turning effect (torque).

ALP.q: The Seebeck effect is the basis of:
ALP.solution: The Seebeck effect (EMF produced by temperature difference at junctions) is the basis of thermocouples.

ALP.q: A concave mirror has a focal length of 10 cm. Its radius of curvature is:
ALP.solution: Radius of curvature R = 2 × focal length = 2 × 10 = 20 cm.

ALP.q: Which type of electromagnetic wave is used in TV remote controls?
ALP.solution: TV remotes use infrared (IR) radiation to send signals to the receiver.

ALP.q: The mass number of an atom is:
ALP.solution: Mass number A = number of protons (Z) + number of neutrons (N).

ALP.q: Maxwell's equations unify which two phenomena?
ALP.solution: Maxwell's four equations unified electricity and magnetism into a single electromagnetic theory.

ALP.q: The process of thermionic emission depends on:
ALP.solution: Thermionic emission (release of electrons from hot metals) depends on the temperature of the metal.

ALP.q: The Brewster angle is the angle at which:
options: Refracted and reflected rays are perpendicular
ALP.solution: At Brewster's angle, reflected light is completely polarised and reflected + refracted rays are 90° apart.

ALP.q: A rheostat is used to:
ALP.solution: A rheostat is a variable resistor used to vary current in a circuit by changing resistance.

ALP.q: The phenomenon of beats is produced when two sound waves of:
ALP.solution: Beats occur when two sounds with slightly different frequencies interfere, producing periodic loudness variation.

ALP.q: Gamma rays, X-rays, UV rays — which has the highest energy?
ALP.solution: Gamma rays have the highest energy (shortest wavelength, highest frequency) among these.

ALP.q: The human body temperature is approximately:
ALP.solution: Normal human body temperature is approximately 37°C (98.6°F).

ALP.q: Which property of light is used in optical fibres?
ALP.solution: Optical fibres use total internal reflection (TIR) to transmit light signals with minimal loss.

ALP.q: The band gap of silicon is approximately:
ALP.solution: Silicon has a band gap of ~1.1 eV, making it a semiconductor at room temperature.

ALP.q: Resistors in series have the same:
ALP.solution: In a series circuit, the same current flows through all resistors.

ALP.q: The Hubble's law states that recession velocity of galaxy is proportional to:
ALP.solution: Hubble's law: v = H₀d — recession velocity is proportional to the galaxy's distance.

ALP.q: A gyroscope maintains its orientation due to:
ALP.solution: A gyroscope resists changes in orientation due to conservation of angular momentum.
